definition: The scientific study of life in the geological past, primarily through the analysis of fossils. Combines biology with geology to understand ancient organisms and their environments.

The preserved remains, impressions, or traces of once-living organisms from a past geological age. Fossils can include bones, teeth, shells, leaf imprints, footprints, and even preserved soft tissues.

The geological era spanning from 252 to 66 million years ago, divided into three periods: Triassic, Jurassic, and Cretaceous. Often called the 'Age of Reptiles' due to the dominance of dinosaurs and other reptiles.
The first period of the Mesozoic Era (252–201 Mya). The first dinosaurs, mammals, and pterosaurs appeared during the Triassic. Pangaea was still largely intact.
The middle period of the Mesozoic Era (201–145 Mya). Characterized by the dominance of sauropods, the diversification of theropods, and the appearance of the first birds.
The final and longest period of the Mesozoic Era (145–66 Mya). Flowering plants appeared, dinosaur diversity peaked, and the period ended with the K-Pg mass extinction.
Abbreviation for 'million years ago'. A standard unit used in geology and paleontology to express deep time. Example: T. rex lived approximately 68–66 Mya.
A member of the suborder Theropoda — bipedal, primarily carnivorous dinosaurs. Includes T. rex, Velociraptor, and all modern birds. Characterized by hollow bones and three-toed limbs.
A member of the suborder Sauropodomorpha — large, long-necked, herbivorous dinosaurs that walked on four legs. Includes the largest land animals ever, such as Argentinosaurus and Diplodocus.

One of the two major orders of dinosaurs, characterized by a pubic bone that points backward (bird-hipped). Includes ceratopsians, hadrosaurs, stegosaurs, ankylosaurs, and pachycephalosaurs. Despite the name, birds are NOT ornithischians.

One of the two major orders of dinosaurs, characterized by a pubic bone that points forward (lizard-hipped). Includes theropods and sauropodomorphs. All birds are saurischians, despite being 'lizard-hipped'.
A group of herbivorous ornithischian dinosaurs characterized by beaked faces, neck frills, and often horns. Includes Triceratops, Styracosaurus, and Protoceratops.

The 'duck-billed dinosaurs' — a diverse family of ornithischian herbivores with broad, flat beaks and complex dental batteries. Many species had elaborate cranial crests. Includes Parasaurolophus and Edmontosaurus.
A group of heavily armoured ornithischian dinosaurs with bony osteoderms embedded in their skin. Many species had tail clubs. Includes Ankylosaurus and Euoplocephalus.
A group of ornithischian dinosaurs characterized by rows of plates and/or spikes along their backs and tails. Includes Stegosaurus and Kentrosaurus. Most diverse during the Late Jurassic.

The 'thick-headed lizards' — a group of bipedal ornithischian dinosaurs with thickened skull roofs. Includes Pachycephalosaurus and Stygimoloch. Their dome-shaped skulls may have been used for display or flank-butting.

The 'raptor' dinosaurs — a family of small to mid-sized feathered theropods with a distinctive sickle-shaped claw on each foot. Includes Velociraptor, Deinonychus, and Utahraptor. Closely related to birds.
A family of large theropod dinosaurs characterized by massive skulls, tiny arms, and powerful bite forces. Includes Tyrannosaurus, Tarbosaurus, Albertosaurus, and Gorgosaurus.
A diverse group of sauropod dinosaurs that includes the largest land animals ever known. Dominant sauropods of the Cretaceous period, found worldwide. Includes Argentinosaurus and Patagotitan.
A family of large theropod dinosaurs with elongated, crocodilian-like snouts adapted for catching fish. Many had sail-like structures on their backs. Includes Spinosaurus, Baryonyx, and Suchomimus.
The 'ostrich-mimic' dinosaurs — a family of fast-running, toothless theropods with long legs and arms. Likely omnivorous. Includes Gallimimus and Ornithomimus.
A family of large predatory theropods named for their shark-like serrated teeth. Among the largest land predators ever. Includes Giganotosaurus, Carcharodontosaurus, and Mapusaurus.

A family of theropod dinosaurs that were the dominant predators of the southern continents (Gondwana) during the Cretaceous. Characterized by short, deep skulls and very small arms. Includes Carnotaurus.
A family of sauropod dinosaurs with very long necks and whip-like tails. Characterized by pencil-shaped teeth used for stripping foliage. Includes Diplodocus, Apatosaurus, and Barosaurus.

A family of sauropod dinosaurs characterized by front legs longer than hind legs, giving an upward-sloping body profile. High browsers that could reach treetop vegetation. Includes Brachiosaurus and Giraffatitan.

The supercontinent that existed during the late Paleozoic and early Mesozoic, comprising all of Earth's major landmasses. Its breakup during the Mesozoic isolated dinosaur populations and drove evolutionary divergence.

The southern supercontinent that formed when Pangaea split apart, comprising what would become South America, Africa, India, Antarctica, and Australia. Its breakup heavily influenced southern hemisphere dinosaur evolution.
The northern supercontinent formed from the breakup of Pangaea, comprising what would become North America, Europe, and Asia. Many iconic Late Cretaceous dinosaurs evolved on Laurasian landmasses.

The Cretaceous-Paleogene mass extinction event 66 million years ago that ended the age of dinosaurs. Caused by the Chicxulub asteroid impact and possibly exacerbated by Deccan Traps volcanism. Eliminated ~75% of all species.

The asteroid impact that struck the Yucatán Peninsula of Mexico 66 million years ago, creating a ~180 km wide crater. The primary trigger of the K-Pg mass extinction that killed the non-avian dinosaurs.

Massive volcanic eruptions in what is now India that occurred around the time of the K-Pg extinction. Released enormous quantities of gases that may have stressed global ecosystems before the asteroid impact.
A widespread, rapid decrease in the biodiversity of life on Earth. The 'Big Five' mass extinctions shaped the history of life; the most recent (K-Pg, 66 Mya) ended the age of dinosaurs.

A bony deposit forming scales, plates, or other structures embedded in the dermis (skin layer). Found in ankylosaurs, stegosaurs, crocodilians, and some other reptiles. Provides protection and can aid thermoregulation.

An organism that generates its own body heat internally through metabolic processes (warm-blooded). Growing evidence suggests many dinosaurs were endothermic or mesothermic, not cold-blooded as once thought.
An organism that relies on external sources of heat to regulate body temperature (cold-blooded). Traditional (now largely outdated) view was that all dinosaurs were ectothermic like modern lizards.

An intermediate metabolic strategy between endothermy and ectothermy. Some researchers propose many dinosaurs were mesotherms — generating some internal heat but not maintaining a constant body temperature.
Walking or moving on two legs. Many dinosaurs were obligate bipeds (theropods) or facultative bipeds (some ornithopods that could walk on two or four legs).
Walking or moving on four legs. Large sauropods, ceratopsians, ankylosaurs, and stegosaurs were obligate quadrupeds, often with robust, column-like limbs for weight support.
An opening or window-like hole in a bone, particularly in the skull. Dinosaur skulls often had several fenestrae to reduce weight while maintaining structural strength. Plural: fenestrae.
The upward-projecting process of a vertebra. Elongated neural spines formed the sails or ridges seen in Spinosaurus, Ouranosaurus, and some other dinosaurs.

A complex arrangement of hundreds of tightly-packed teeth in rows and columns, found in hadrosaurs and ceratopsians. Worn teeth were continuously replaced by new ones growing beneath, creating an efficient grinding surface.
Belly ribs — bony elements in the abdominal wall of some archosaurs, including many theropod dinosaurs. Not attached to the spine like true ribs; may have aided breathing.
The fused clavicles (wishbone) found in theropod dinosaurs and modern birds. Its presence in non-avian theropods was key evidence for the evolutionary link between dinosaurs and birds.

The presence of air-filled spaces within bones, connected to the respiratory system. Found in theropods and sauropods, it reduced skeletal weight and was linked to an efficient bird-like breathing system.
A group of organisms consisting of an ancestor and all of its descendants — a complete branch on the tree of life. Dinosauria is a clade; so is Aves (birds), which is nested within it.
The evolutionary history and relationships among organisms or groups. Depicted as branching tree diagrams (phylogenetic trees), they show how species are related through common ancestors.
The study of how organisms decay, become fossilized, and are preserved in the geological record. Understanding taphonomy is essential for interpreting fossil sites and diagnosing preservation biases.
The study of rock layers (strata) and their relationships. Stratigraphic analysis determines the relative ages of fossil-bearing rocks and helps establish when different dinosaurs lived.
Using fossils to correlate and date rock layers. Certain index fossils are associated with specific time intervals, allowing geologists to determine the age of surrounding strata.
A method of determining the age of rocks or fossils using the known decay rates of radioactive isotopes. Potassium-argon and uranium-lead dating are commonly used for dinosaur-bearing rocks.

A famous Late Jurassic geological formation in western North America (155–148 Mya). One of the richest dinosaur fossil sites, yielding Allosaurus, Stegosaurus, Diplodocus, Brachiosaurus, and many others.

A Late Cretaceous geological formation (67–66 Mya) in Montana, Wyoming, and the Dakotas. Preserves the last dinosaur ecosystems before the K-Pg extinction, including T. rex, Triceratops, and Edmontosaurus.
An Early Cretaceous geological formation in Liaoning Province, China (~125–120 Mya). Famous for exceptionally preserved feathered dinosaurs including Microraptor and Sinornithosaurus.

A Late Jurassic fossil site in Tanzania, East Africa. Massive German expeditions (1909–1913) recovered Giraffatitan (then called Brachiosaurus), Kentrosaurus, and other dinosaurs. The Berlin Giraffatitan mount comes from here.
A Late Cretaceous formation in Alberta, Canada (~76.5–75 Mya). One of the richest dinosaur sites in the world, yielding diverse hadrosaurs, ceratopsians, tyrannosaurs, and ankylosaurs.
A site in New Mexico where hundreds of Coelophysis skeletons were found in a single bonebed, one of the richest accumulations of Triassic dinosaur fossils.
The microscopic study of bone tissue. Growth rings in dinosaur bones reveal information about growth rates, age at death, metabolic rates, and whether the animal was still growing.
A cellular organelle containing melanin pigment. Fossilized melanosomes preserved in feathered dinosaur specimens allow scientists to reconstruct the original colours of dinosaur plumage.
The study of trace fossils — tracks, burrows, and other evidence of biological activity. Dinosaur ichnology uses trackways to study locomotion, speed, behaviour, and group dynamics.
A series of footprints left by an animal as it moves. Dinosaur trackways are trace fossils that provide information about locomotion, speed, posture, and social behaviour.
Fossilized feces. Dinosaur coprolites provide direct evidence of diet, containing identifiable fragments of food. Some T. rex coprolites contain crushed bone fragments.
Stones deliberately swallowed by an animal to aid in digestion by grinding food in the gizzard. Found associated with some sauropod and ornithomimid dinosaur skeletons.
The single physical specimen upon which the formal scientific description and name of a new species is based. Every named dinosaur species has a designated holotype specimen.
The two-part system for naming species: genus name + species name (e.g., Tyrannosaurus rex). Established by Carl Linnaeus and used throughout biology, including paleontology.

A method of classifying organisms based on shared derived characteristics (synapomorphies). Modern dinosaur classification relies heavily on cladistic analysis to determine evolutionary relationships.
A shared derived characteristic found in two or more groups that is inherited from their most recent common ancestor. Used in cladistic analysis to determine evolutionary relationships.

The independent evolution of similar features in unrelated lineages due to similar environmental pressures. Example: the similar body plans of ichthyosaurs and dolphins, or similar domes in pachycephalosaurs.

The rapid diversification of a lineage into many new forms, typically following the extinction of competing groups or the colonization of new environments. Dinosaurs underwent adaptive radiation after the end-Triassic extinction.

The process by which coexisting species use different ecological resources or strategies to minimize competition. Example: Spinosaurus and Carcharodontosaurus occupying aquatic vs. terrestrial predator roles.

Physical differences between males and females of the same species, beyond reproductive organs. In dinosaurs, this may include crest size (Parasaurolophus), frill elaboration (Protoceratops), or body size.

The developmental history of an individual organism from embryo to adult. Understanding dinosaur ontogeny helps scientists recognize that juveniles and adults can look dramatically different, preventing mis-naming as separate species.

The arrangement of four spikes on the tail of stegosaurid dinosaurs. The term was coined by cartoonist Gary Larson in a 1982 Far Side cartoon and subsequently adopted by paleontologists as an informal anatomical term.

A member of the clade Archosauria, which includes crocodilians, pterosaurs, dinosaurs, and birds. Archosaurs are distinguished by features like antorbital fenestrae (skull openings) and a specialized ankle joint.

Flying reptiles of the order Pterosauria, closely related to dinosaurs but not dinosaurs themselves. Ranged from sparrow-sized to Quetzalcoatlus with a 10+ metre wingspan. Extinct at the K-Pg boundary.
Marine reptiles of the order Plesiosauria, with long necks and paddle-like limbs. Not dinosaurs, but contemporary marine predators. Ranged from 2 to 15 metres long. Extinct at the K-Pg boundary.
Marine reptiles of the order Ichthyosauria, with dolphin-like body plans. Not dinosaurs. They went extinct before the K-Pg boundary, declining during the mid-Cretaceous.
Any dinosaur belonging to the clade Aves — modern birds and their closest extinct relatives. Birds are technically dinosaurs, meaning dinosaurs did not go completely extinct at the K-Pg boundary.
Any dinosaur that is not a member of Aves (birds). When people say 'dinosaurs went extinct', they mean non-avian dinosaurs. Over 1,000 non-avian species have been described.

The geological boundary between the Cretaceous and Paleogene periods, dated to exactly 66 million years ago. Marked by the K-Pg mass extinction, an iridium-rich layer, and the disappearance of non-avian dinosaur fossils.

An unusually high concentration of the element iridium found in a thin clay layer at the K-Pg boundary worldwide. Iridium is rare on Earth's surface but common in asteroids, providing key evidence for the Chicxulub impact.

A hypothesized period of global cooling following a large asteroid impact. Dust and soot ejected into the atmosphere would block sunlight, collapse photosynthesis, and cause widespread ecosystem collapse. Proposed mechanism for K-Pg extinction.
A geological deposit containing a high concentration of fossil bones. Mono-specific bonebeds (containing one species) suggest mass death events and can provide evidence of herd behaviour.

A geological deposit with exceptionally preserved fossils, including soft tissues rarely fossilized. The Solnhofen Limestone (Archaeopteryx) and Yixian Formation (feathered dinosaurs) are famous Lagerstätten.

The most common type of fossilization, in which mineral-rich groundwater infiltrates porous tissues like bone, filling spaces with minerals. The original structure is preserved while being reinforced with stone.
A type of fossilization where the original organism dissolves, leaving an impression (mold) that can later be filled with minerals to create a replica (cast).
Indirect evidence of an organism's existence, such as footprints, burrows, nests, coprolites, or tooth marks. Trace fossils record behaviour rather than anatomy.

The preserved physical remains of an organism — bones, teeth, shells, etc. Distinguished from trace fossils, which record behaviour. Most of what we know about dinosaur anatomy comes from body fossils.

In paleontology, the careful process of removing rock matrix from around a fossil using tools ranging from dental picks to air scribes to acid baths. Skilled preparators can spend months or years on a single specimen.
A fossil skeleton in which the bones are found in their natural anatomical positions. Much rarer and more scientifically valuable than disarticulated (scattered) remains.
A grouping of dinosaur nests found together, indicating social nesting behaviour. The Maiasaura nesting grounds in Montana provided the first evidence that dinosaurs cared for their young.
An organism that lays eggs in the nest of another species. Some oviraptorids may have displayed brooding behaviour similar to modern birds, sitting on nests to incubate eggs.
A cast of the interior of a skull, revealing the shape and size of the brain and associated structures. Dinosaur endocasts help scientists estimate intelligence, sensory abilities, and behaviour.

Computed tomography — a technique using X-rays to create detailed 3D images of fossil interiors without destructive sampling. Revolutionized paleontology by revealing internal skull structures, brain shapes, and inner ear anatomy.

A method of inferring unknown traits in extinct organisms by examining their closest living relatives. Since crocodilians and birds bracket dinosaurs phylogenetically, shared features of both groups were likely present in dinosaurs.

A fundamental shift in paleontological thinking beginning in the 1960s-70s, largely sparked by John Ostrom's work on Deinonychus. Changed the view of dinosaurs from sluggish, cold-blooded failures to active, warm-blooded animals.

A clade of theropod dinosaurs that includes birds and their closest non-avian relatives: dromaeosaurids, oviraptorids, troodontids, and therizinosaurids. Characterised by long arms and the development of feathers.
A major clade of theropod dinosaurs that includes tyrannosaurs, ornithomimids, maniraptorans, and birds. Most coelurosaurs were feathered, and they represent the most diverse theropod group.

The outer covering of an organism. In dinosaurs, integument could include scales, scutes, osteoderms, feathers, or combinations thereof. Preserved integument provides crucial data about appearance and function.
A bone containing internal air spaces connected to the respiratory system. Found in theropods and sauropods, these light but strong bones enabled large body size and efficient breathing.

Annual or seasonal lines of arrested growth visible in dinosaur bone cross-sections, similar to tree rings. Counting growth rings provides minimum age estimates; spacing indicates growth rate changes.
A large inland sea that split North America during the Cretaceous (100–70 Mya), from the Gulf of Mexico to the Arctic Ocean. Divided dinosaur populations into eastern and western faunas.

The formal taxonomic name for the group containing all dinosaurs — both the saurischians (including birds) and ornithischians. Defined by Richard Owen in 1842. Currently includes over 1,000 described non-avian species.

The debated hypothesis that T. rex was primarily a scavenger rather than an active predator. Multiple lines of evidence (binocular vision, speed, healed bite marks on living prey) now strongly support an active predator role.

The evolutionary reduction in body size of large animals confined to islands with limited resources. Examples in dinosaurs include the small sauropod Europasaurus and the dwarf hadrosaur Tethyshadros on Cretaceous European islands.

The maintenance of stable, warm body temperatures through large body mass alone, without endothermic metabolism. Large sauropods may have been gigantotherms — their sheer size prevented rapid heat loss.
Having different types of teeth in the jaw (incisors, canines, molars, etc.), adapted for different functions. Heterodontosaurus is named for this feature, unusual among ornithischians.
Having teeth of uniform shape throughout the jaw. Many dinosaurs, including most theropods and sauropods, had homodont dentition — all teeth served the same function.

The suborder of saurischian dinosaurs comprising all bipedal, primarily carnivorous forms. Includes all meat-eating dinosaurs from Coelophysis to T. rex, as well as all birds. The most diverse dinosaur group.
The suborder of saurischian dinosaurs comprising the long-necked herbivores. Ranges from relatively small prosauropods like Plateosaurus to the titanic sauropods like Argentinosaurus.
The 'shield bearers' — the armoured dinosaur clade containing both stegosaurs and ankylosaurs. Characterized by rows of bony osteoderms along the back. An ornithischian subgroup.
A clade of ornithischian dinosaurs characterized by a bony shelf or frill on the back of the skull. Includes ceratopsians (horned dinosaurs) and pachycephalosaurs (dome-headed dinosaurs).

A clade of maniraptorans that includes dromaeosaurids, troodontids, and birds. Paravians evolved powered flight or gliding multiple times, and most are characterized by large sickle-shaped foot claws.

The group containing modern crocodilians and their extinct relatives. As archosaurs, crocodylomorphs are the closest living relatives of dinosaurs and birds. They diversified alongside dinosaurs throughout the Mesozoic.
